title: What grit for bamboo before oil?
problem: Bamboo can look uneven or streaky when oil is applied after rough or inconsistent sanding.
likely_cause: Bamboo is dense and can show sanding direction and uneven scratch patterns under oil.
recommended_grit: Use 180, then 220. Stop at 220 before oil for a clean, even surface.
steps: Finish with 220 grit in the grain direction where possible.
avoid: Do not over-polish bamboo with ultra-fine grit before oil.
success_check: The bamboo looks evenly dull and feels smooth before oil.
quick_answer: For bamboo before oil, use 180 then 220. Clean all dust before applying oil.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use heavy pressure that creates shiny low spots.
Do not apply oil over sanding dust.
search_phrases: what grit for bamboo before oil

title: Can I sand cork?
problem: Cork can tear, crumble, or become uneven when sanded aggressively.
likely_cause: Cork is soft and porous, so coarse grits and heavy pressure remove material unevenly.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 only for very light smoothing. Avoid coarse grits.
steps: Test on a hidden area first.
Use 320 or 400 with very light pressure.
Sand only raised spots or rough edges.
avoid: Do not sand cork with coarse grit or heavy pressure.
success_check: The cork remains flat and intact with no torn edges.
quick_answer: Cork should only be sanded lightly. Use 320 or 400 and avoid heavy pressure.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use 80, 120, or aggressive sanding on cork.
Do not sand decorative cork faces without testing first.

title: What grit for OSB before painting?
problem: OSB can show rough strands and raised texture after paint.
likely_cause: OSB has uneven wood strands that need light leveling, but aggressive sanding can tear the surface.
recommended_grit: Use 120 for rough spots, then 180 or 220 before primer.
steps: Sand high rough spots with 120 grit.
avoid: Do not try to make OSB look like smooth plywood with sanding alone.
success_check: The OSB is cleaner, duller, and free of loose raised fibers before primer.
quick_answer: For OSB before painting, use 120 on rough spots, then 180 or 220 before primer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not oversand edges until strands lift.
Do not paint over loose OSB dust.
search_phrases: what grit for OSB before painting

title: What grit for particle board edges before painting?
problem: Particle board edges can stay rough and absorb paint unevenly.
likely_cause: Open particle board edges are porous and fragile, so they need gentle smoothing and sealing before paint.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for edges. After sealer or primer dries, scuff with 320.
steps: Sand the raw edge lightly with 180 or 220.
After it dries, scuff lightly with 320 before paint.
avoid: Do not sand particle board edges aggressively.
success_check: The edge feels smoother and sealed before the paint coat.
quick_answer: Use 180 or 220 on particle board edges, seal or prime, then scuff with 320 before paint.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not round over the edge with heavy pressure.
Do not paint raw dusty particle board edges.
search_phrases: what grit for particle board edges

title: What grit for MDF edges before sealing?
problem: MDF edges stay fuzzy or rough after primer if they are not prepared correctly.
likely_cause: MDF edges absorb sealer and primer more than the face, and the fibers can raise during prep.
recommended_grit: Use 220 before sealing, then 320 after the sealer or primer dries.
steps: Sand the MDF edge with 220 grit.
Apply sealer or primer to the edge.
Scuff the dry sealed edge with 320.
avoid: Do not paint raw fuzzy MDF edges without sealing.
success_check: The edge feels smooth and sealed before the topcoat.
quick_answer: For MDF edges, sand with 220, seal or prime, then scuff with 320 after it dries.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not soak MDF edges with water.
Do not use coarse grit that tears the edge fibers.
search_phrases: what grit for MDF edges before sealing

title: What grit for plywood before clear coat?
problem: Plywood can show sanding scratches or blotchy areas under clear finish.
likely_cause: Thin plywood face veneer can scratch or sand through easily, so the grit sequence must be controlled.
recommended_grit: Use 180, then 220 before clear coat. Use 320 only for light scuffing between cured coats.
steps: Sand the plywood face lightly with 180.
Use 320 only between cured clear coats.
avoid: Do not sand through the thin plywood face veneer.
success_check: The plywood face is smooth and has no visible sanding-through marks.
quick_answer: For plywood before clear coat, use 180 then 220. Use 320 only between cured coats.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not start with coarse grit on plywood face veneer.
Do not press hard on edges or corners.
search_phrases: what grit for plywood before clear coat

title: What grit removes crayon from wood?
problem: Crayon marks can smear or stay in the wood grain after wiping.
likely_cause: Crayon is waxy, so sanding too aggressively can spread residue or create visible low spots.
recommended_grit: Use 220 for light marks. Use 180 first only when the mark is deeper, then finish with 220.
steps: Scrape or wipe off excess crayon first.
Finish the area again with 220 and clean dust.
avoid: Do not grind waxy crayon residue deep into the grain.
success_check: The mark is removed and the surrounding wood has an even scratch pattern.
quick_answer: For crayon on wood, remove excess first, then use 220. Use 180 only for stubborn spots.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not start with 80 or 120 for small crayon marks.
Do not leave wax residue before finishing.
search_phrases: what grit removes crayon from wood

title: What grit for pet scratches on wood floor?
problem: Pet scratches can remain visible after light sanding on wood flooring.
likely_cause: Floor scratches often vary in depth. Light scratches need blending; deeper scratches may need refinishing beyond spot sanding.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for light blending. Use 120 only for deeper visible scratches, then refine to 180 and 220.
steps: Start with 180 or 220 for light scratches.
Use 120 only if the scratch is still visible.
Blend the area with 180 and 220 before finishing.
avoid: Do not spot sand a deep low area into the floor.
success_check: The scratch is blended and the repair area does not show a hard sanding edge.
quick_answer: For light pet scratches, use 180 or 220. Use 120 only for deeper scratches, then refine to 220.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand through the floor finish without planning the refinish step.
Do not leave a visible oval spot around the repair.
search_phrases: what grit for pet scratches on wood floor

title: What grit for screening hardwood floors?
problem: Hardwood floor finish needs light scuffing before a new coat, not aggressive removal.
likely_cause: Screening is meant to abrade the existing finish for adhesion. Too coarse a grit can cut through the finish unevenly.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 for light scuff sanding between floor finish coats.
steps: Scuff the cured finish lightly with 220 or 320.
Vacuum and tack the dust completely.
Apply the next compatible finish coat.
avoid: Do not use coarse grit when only screening a finish coat.
success_check: The finish is evenly dulled with no sand-through spots.
quick_answer: For screening hardwood floors, use 220 or 320 lightly on the cured finish before recoating.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not screen a dirty floor.
Do not sand through the finish into bare wood by accident.
search_phrases: what grit for screening hardwood floors

title: What grit for stair railing before stain?
problem: Stair railings can stain unevenly or show sanding scratches.
likely_cause: Curved rails and hand contact areas often have old finish, oils, and uneven sanding marks.
recommended_grit: Use 150 or 180 for old finish cleanup, then 220 before stain.
steps: Clean hand oils and residue from the railing.
Sand old rough areas with 150 or 180.
Finish with 220 along the length of the rail.
avoid: Do not stain over glossy or oily hand-contact areas.
success_check: The railing feels smooth and evenly dull before stain.
quick_answer: For stair railing before stain, clean first, use 150 or 180, then finish with 220.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not leave old glossy finish on the handrail.
Do not skip dust removal before stain.
search_phrases: what grit for stair railing before stain

title: What grit for handrail before polyurethane?
problem: A handrail can feel rough or show scratches under polyurethane.
likely_cause: Handrails get oils and wear from hands, so the surface must be clean, smooth, and evenly scuffed before finish.
recommended_grit: Use 180 then 220 before the first coat. Use 320 or 400 lightly between cured coats.

Scuff cured coats lightly with 320 or 400.
avoid: Do not apply polyurethane over oily or dusty handrail surfaces.
success_check: The handrail is smooth, clean, and evenly prepared before finish.
quick_answer: For handrail before polyurethane, use 180 then 220. Between cured coats, scuff with 320 or 400.

title: What grit after pressure washing a deck?
problem: Deck boards can feel fuzzy or raised after pressure washing.
likely_cause: Water and pressure can raise wood fibers, especially on soft or weathered deck boards.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for raised fibers, then 150 if a smoother stain-ready surface is needed.
wet_or_dry: Dry after the deck is fully dry.
steps: Clean dust before stain or sealer.
avoid: Do not sand wet deck boards.
success_check: The deck feels even and dry with no fuzzy raised grain before finishing.

mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use 220+ as the main deck leveling grit.

title: Fence still has splinters after sanding
problem: Fence boards can still feel splintery after light sanding.
likely_cause: Weathered fence wood often has raised fibers and damaged grain that needs a coarse enough grit before stain prep.

title: What grit between cabinet primer coats?
problem: Cabinet primer can feel rough, dusty, or uneven between coats.
likely_cause: Primer often raises fibers or leaves small dust nibs that need light scuff sanding before the next coat.

title: What grit removes dust nibs in cabinet paint?
problem: Small bumps or dust nibs show in cabinet paint after a coat dries.
likely_cause: Dust can land in wet paint, and the raised nibs need light leveling after the coat cures.
recommended_grit: Use 600 for light dust nibs. Use 400 only when the nibs are more noticeable, then refine with 600.

Use 400 only for larger raised nibs.

title: Should I sand greasy cabinets?
problem: Greasy cabinets can still peel after sanding and painting.
likely_cause: Sandpaper can spread grease into the scratch pattern instead of removing it, which weakens primer or paint adhesion.

avoid: Do not sand grease into the cabinet surface.

title: How to avoid sanding through clear coat edges
problem: Clear coat can sand through quickly on edges, corners, and body lines.
likely_cause: Edges have less film thickness and receive more pressure from the paper, so they cut faster than flat areas.
recommended_grit: Use 1500, 2000, or 3000 near edges. Avoid coarse grits on clear coat edges.
steps: Tape or protect sharp edges when possible.
Wet sand with 1500 or finer near body lines.
Polish after the haze is uniform.
avoid: Do not sand clear coat edges with aggressive pressure.
success_check: The flat area is refined without exposed color or sand-through on edges.
